senses.definition: People who are devoted to scholarship or education, often academics or students considered as a group.
examples: The conference brought together intellectuals and scholastics from many universities.
She moved in scholarly circles, admired by fellow scholastics.

senses.definition: A Latin term used historically to refer to a schoolmaster or scholar; rarely used in modern English except in historical or literary contexts.
examples: The term scholasticus appears in medieval texts to denote an academic official.
In the sermon, the scholasticus is invoked as a figure of learned authority.

senses.definition: Scholia are marginal notes or comments added to ancient texts by scholars; they can also refer to scholarly commentary in general.
examples: The manuscript contains scholia that explain difficult passages.
Scholia on Homer have guided readers for centuries.

senses.definition: A person who writes scholia; a commentator or annotator of ancient texts.
examples: The scholiast provided notes explaining the mythological references.
Scholiasts contributed valuable insights to classical literature.

senses.definition: Relating to scholia or to the practice of annotating texts; pertaining to scholarly commentary.
examples: The scholiastic notes clarified the obscure passage.
Her scholiastic approach enriched the annotation of the manuscript.

senses.definition: plural of scholiast; multiple commentators who write scholia.
examples: The scholiasts of antiquity offered diverse explanations.
Scholiasts contributed to the critical apparatus of the text.

senses.definition: A brief note or marginal gloss in a classical manuscript; a short scholarly remark.
examples: The scholion explained a difficult term in the text.
Manuscripts often contain many scholia and scholions.

senses.definition: A brief note in a text that explains, clarifies, or adds information; also a scholarly digression.
examples: The editor inserted a scholium to define the term.
The scholium provides a helpful aside about the historical context.

senses.definition: Uncertain or erroneous coinage; appears to be a misspelling or nonstandard plural form related to scholium or scholia.
examples: The manuscript lists 'scholiumlia' in the notes, likely a mistake.
Scholiumlia is not a recognized term in standard reference works.

senses.definition: Plural of scholium; brief notes or digressions in texts that provide explanation or commentary.
examples: The manuscript is full of scholiums that illuminate the main argument.
Scholiums help readers understand obscure jargon.

senses.definition: A genus of orchid plants; commonly used in horticulture and botany.
examples: Schomburgkia orchids are prized by collectors.
The greenhouse hosts several Schomburgkia varieties.

senses.definition: Variant spelling or proper noun form; may appear as a surname or place name in Germanic contexts.
examples: Schöne is a common surname in German-speaking regions.
The village of Schöne lies along the river.

senses.definition: A rare mineral or rock type; used in geological contexts.
examples: Schonfelsite is studied by mineralogists for its unique composition.

senses.definition: A place name, such as Schoodic Peninsula in Maine; used as a proper noun.
examples: We visited Schoodic while touring coastal Maine.
Schoodic Peninsula offers rugged ocean views.

senses.definition: A place where children go to learn; a group of students taught together; a system of education.
examples: The school opens at eight o'clock.
She works at a local school.
The school has a strong music program.

senses.definition: To educate or train someone; to fit someone for school or work; to cause to learn.
examples: Parents school their children in honesty and responsibility.
The program aims to school young athletes in discipline.

senses.definition: Suitable for attending or being taught in a school; capable of being educated.
examples: The curriculum is designed to be schoolable for primary students.
Not all digital games are schoolable for classroom use.

senses.definition: The period of life when a person is old enough to go to school; typically ages from around 5 to 18.
examples: Schoolage children need good nutrition and rest.
The library offers programs for schoolage readers.

senses.definition: A bag used by a student to carry books and supplies to school.
examples: She packed her books into the schoolbag.
His schoolbag was heavy with notebooks.

senses.definition: Plural of schoolbag; multiple bags carried by students.
examples: The students lined up with their schoolbags ready for class.
Different designs of schoolbags were on display.

senses.definition: A book used for teaching and learning in school; a textbook.
examples: She forgot her schoolbook at home.
The teacher recommended a new science schoolbook.

senses.definition: In the style of a schoolbook; overly formal or didactic; resembling textbook prose.
examples: The essay took on a schoolbookish tone that stifled its voice.
A schoolbookish approach may hinder creative expression.

senses.definition: Multiple textbooks or course books used for school learning.
examples: The desk was piled with schoolbooks for the new semester.
She borrowed several schoolbooks from the library.

senses.definition: A boy who attends school; a male student.
examples: The schoolboy walked home with his backpack.
Schoolboys gathered in the courtyard during recess.

senses.definition: The condition or world of being a schoolboy; not a common modern word, mainly historical or humorous usage.
examples: In tales of old, schoolboydom is depicted with strict routines.

senses.definition: The period during which someone is a schoolboy; childhood as a student.
examples: He fondly remembered his schoolboyhood in the small town.
